behaviors.description: Command segmentation treats newline-separated statements as separate segments, not one monolithic block.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is local parsing/segmentation logic on an input string, with no required I/O. MOCK-MISS check: this behavior itself is about one algorithmic boundary, not cross-module handoff. Rejected higher level: behavior text is strictly about 'segmentation' and does not require OS/network/subprocess (so not System) or any AI output (so not Agentic/Workflow).
behaviors.test_description: Call the segmenter with a multi-line shell snippet containing assignments and a later command; assert returned segments are split per newline statement boundaries and preserve order.

behaviors.description: A multi-line shell block with variable assignments followed by `gh pr create` is recognized as a PR-create trigger.
behaviors.justification: Real failure appears at module handoff: segmentation output must be consumed correctly by trigger classification. A unit test on matcher-only logic could miss the incident pattern where the whole block was classified `not_a_trigger`. Rejected higher level: behavior text is about command text recognition, not real subprocess execution of `gh` (so System disqualified), and no AI dependency (Agentic/Workflow disqualified).
behaviors.test_description: Run the hook classification pipeline end-to-end in-process on a multi-line command block (`VAR=...` lines then `gh pr create ...`); assert final classification is PR-create trigger, not `not_a_trigger`.

behaviors.description: A similar multi-line block that ends with `gh issue create` is not incorrectly treated as a PR-create trigger.
behaviors.justification: This is the negative pair for the same cross-module path (segmenter + trigger classifier), validating precision at the handoff boundary. MOCK-MISS: isolated unit matching can miss misclassification caused by upstream segmentation context. Rejected higher level: behavior text only differentiates CLI subcommands in parsed text, not real `gh` process behavior (so not System) and not AI-driven (not Agentic/Workflow).
behaviors.test_description: Feed a multi-line block with variable assignments and terminal `gh issue create ...` through the same in-process hook pipeline; assert result is non-PR trigger classification.

behaviors.description: When a PR-create trigger is detected, the hook can reconstruct and persist the PR identifier needed by the review loop state.
behaviors.justification: Requires wiring classifier result to state-construction and persistence layers, including local filesystem write/read. Integration is sufficient because failure can be reproduced with local temp filesystem and in-process modules. Rejected System: behavior text says 'persist' state, but does not require real subprocess/HTTP/OS event semantics beyond normal local file I/O.
behaviors.test_description: Given a detected PR-create event payload, execute hook persistence path using a temp directory; assert persisted state contains reconstructed PR identifier fields required by the review loop.

behaviors.description: After PR-create detection, the review gate state is written in a form that the pre-tool enforcement hook can read and enforce.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is inter-module contract compatibility (writer hook -> reader/enforcer hook) via shared state format. This is beyond single-function logic, but does not inherently require real OS/network/subprocess. Rejected higher levels: no model output dependence (not Agentic/Workflow); behavior text focuses on readable/enforceable state format, which can be verified in-process with local filesystem fixtures (thus System not required).
behaviors.test_description: In one test flow, invoke post-tool hook logic to write gate state after PR-create detection, then invoke pre-tool enforcement hook against that state; assert it successfully parses and enforces expected gate behavior.
